summary: Problem 3 asks for the construction of a nontrivial Markov chain on restricted distinct-part partitions whose stationary distribution is expressible via interpolation ASEP polynomials and interpolation Macdonald polynomials at q=1. The current best attempt is an interactive solve that was stopped without producing a solution.tex, and the solvability score (13%) reflects that this requires genuinely new mathematical construction rather than routine assembly of known results.
problems: No solution.tex was ever produced; the interactive solve (claude-opus) was stopped before any concrete construction emerged.
Core mathematical blocker: no explicit Markov chain dynamics (transition rules/rates) have been proposed on the state space S_n(lambda) of restricted distinct-part partitions.

The target stationary distribution — a ratio involving interpolation ASEP polynomials and interpolation Macdonald polynomials at q=1 — is not yet formalized or verified to be a valid probability distribution (nonnegativity, normalization).
No reversibility or detailed-balance argument has been attempted to link any candidate chain to the claimed stationary measure.
Specialized prerequisite machinery (interpolation Macdonald polynomials, ASEP polynomial theory) is not summarized or staged, leaving the proof with no foundational scaffolding.
highlights: The problem is precisely scoped: state space (restricted partitions with distinct parts, unique part 0, no part 1) and target distribution family are clearly specified, giving a concrete construction target.
The issue tracking shows the prior solve attempt was cleanly closed/resolved as 'no solution', so there is no misleading partial artifact to untangle.
Strong domain anchor (Lauren Williams' ASEP/Macdonald program) means relevant published constructions exist as templates for designing the chain.

suggested_todos.title: Compile a literature scaffold on interpolation Macdonald polynomials and ASEP polynomials at q=1, extracting known Markov chains with Macdonald-type stationary measures as templates
Formalize the proposed stationary distribution as an explicit ratio and verify nonnegativity and normalization over S_n(lambda) for small n
Propose candidate transition rules (e.g., hopping/exclusion-style moves on distinct parts) and test detailed balance against the target measure for n=2,3
Run small-case computer-algebra experiments to confirm the candidate chain's stationary distribution matches the polynomial ratio before attempting a general proof
Draft a solution.tex skeleton with sections for state space, chain definition, stationarity proof, and nontriviality argument to capture progress incrementally
